title: ROLE OF PHOSPHORYLATION IN THE LINKER 2 REGION  OF POLYPYRIMIDINE TRACT BINDING PROTEIN 2
author: Cuellar, Andres D.
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Polypyrimidine Tract Binding Proteins PTBP1 and PTBP2 are paralogous RNA binding proteins that alternatively splice exons during pre-mRNA processing. The two proteins share high primary sequence identity and similar tertiary structure but have tissue specific expression patterns and regulate overlapping and distinct sets of target exons. How related proteins in a gene family can exert different splicing activities on regulated exons is not understood. Under splicing assay conditions PTBP2 Linker 2 region’s T298 and S308 are phosphorylated post translationally. The corresponding amino acids in PTBP1 are those that cannot undergo phosphorylation. A comparison of PTBP1 and 2 amino acid sequences in the Linker 2 region showed conservation of the corresponding residues across several jawed vertebrates suggesting amino acid changes between the paralogs may have been acquired for functional specialization. Furthermore, a chimera containing PTBP1 Linker 2 region in an otherwise PTBP2 protein showed exon repression like that of wild type PTBP1. We evaluated the hypothesis that PTBP2 phosphorylation at T298 and S308 affects the RNA binding and splicing activity of PTBP2. To evaluate this, we created alanine and phosphomimic single and double mutants of T298 and S308. We assayed their binding affinity via Electrophoretic Mobility Shift Assays (EMSA) to two RNA substrates. We show that double phosphate mimic PTBP2T298DS308D shows significantly higher binding affinity (76 ± 6.0 nM) than PTBP2 wild type (104 ± 7.1 nM) to a region downstream of a regulated exon. Collectively, our studies support a role for phosphorylation in PTBP2 RNA binding affinity.
